1: wayyišlaḥ YHWH et-nāṯān el-dawid: 'YHWH sent Nathan to David.' The prophetic messenger formula establishes Nathan's authority as YHWH's emissary. The form he uses is a māšāl — a comparison story or parable — which draws the listener in as a judge before revealing him as the defendant. This rhetorical strategy of concealed application is distinctive in OT prophetic literature and is replicated by Jesus in several confrontational parables (Matt 21:33-46).
2: 'The rich man had very many flocks and herds.' The parable's economic realism — detailed distribution of assets — grounds the moral point in material terms. The disproportion between the rich man's holdings and the poor man's single lamb is established before the occasion for sin arises. ṣōʾn ûḇāqār harbēh məʾōd (very many small and large livestock) — the abundance that makes the theft more egregious, not less.
3: 'But the poor man had only one little ewe lamb (kiḇśâ ʾaḥat qəṭannâ).' The adjectives accumulate — ʾaḥat (one, only), qəṭannâ (small) — and the lamb is described in terms of household intimacy: bought (wayyiqqānehā), raised by him (wayḥayyehā), grew up with his children, ate his food, drank from his cup, lay in his arms. The term kəḇitô (like his daughter) personalizes the attachment. The poor man's attachment to the lamb is the OT's way of expressing what genuine love for another looks like — not possessive desire but invested, relational care.
4: 'A traveler (ʾôrēaḥ) came to the rich man.' The arrival of a guest creates the moral occasion. Hospitality was an absolute social obligation in the ancient Near East; failing to honor a guest was a serious breach. The rich man wayyaḥmōl (spared) his own flock — the same verb used for Saul's sparing of Agag and the best livestock in 1 Sam 15:9, where David's predecessor was condemned for the same word. The rich man appropriates the poor man's lamb for the guest rather than using his own vast resources.
5: 'David burned with anger (wayyiḥar ʾap dāwid) against the man and said to Nathan: As YHWH lives, the man who has done this deserves to die (ben-māweṯ haʾîš haʿōśeh zōʾt).' David invokes the oath formula ḥay YHWH (as YHWH lives) in pronouncing a capital verdict on the parable's subject — a verdict he does not know he is pronouncing on himself. Ben-māweṯ (son of death/deserving death) is the legal formula for a capital offense in Israel.
6: 'He shall restore the lamb fourfold (wəʾeṯ-hakkiḇśâ yəšallēm ʾarbaʿtāyim), because he did this thing, and because he had no pity.' The fourfold restitution cites Exod 22:1 — the specific penalty for stealing a sheep. David's legal precision is ironic: he applies the Torah's restitution law with confidence, not knowing that Nathan's parable maps directly onto his own actions. The clause ʾăšer lo-ḥāmal (because he had no pity) echoes the verb from v4 — the rich man showed no mercy; David's verdict identifies this pitilessness as the heart of the offense.
7: attah ha-ish: 'You are the man!' The three-word accusation is the most dramatic prophetic confrontation in the OT. The switch from third person (the man in the parable) to second person (you) collapses the hermeneutical distance David had maintained. Nathan then cites YHWH's gifts: anointing as king, deliverance from Saul, the house and wives of his predecessor, the kingdom of Israel and Judah. The inventory of gifts makes the sin not just a legal violation but an act of ingratitude against specific, enumerated divine generosity.
8: 'I gave you your master's house and your master's wives into your arms... and if that were too little, I would add to you as much more (wəʾōsipāh lākā kāhēnnâ wəḵāhēnnâ).' The divine willingness to have given more is the counterfactual that indicts: the sin was not committed from deprivation. wəʾōsipāh — the Hiphil of yāsap (to add/increase) — indicates that YHWH's giving was not exhausted; David could have asked and received. The sin was committed against one who had given everything and would have given more.
9: bāzîṯā dəḇar-YHWH: 'You have despised the word (dāḇār) of YHWH.' The verb bāzâ means to treat as contemptible, to regard with disdain. The sin is characterized not first as a crime against Uriah but as contempt for YHWH's command. Three charges follow: hikkîṯā ḇəḥereḇ (you struck him with the sword), wattiqaḥ ʾet-ʾišˀtô lǝḵā lěʾišˀâ (you took his wife for yourself), wahărağtô ḇəḥereḇ ḇənê ʿammôn (you killed him by the Ammonites' sword). The three charges are presented in narrative order — the taking of Bathsheba (ch11:4) and the arranging of Uriah's death (11:14-15) — but the judgment places the murder last, as the escalating climax.
10: 'Now therefore the sword shall never depart from your house (lō-ṯāsûr ḥereḇ mibêṯəḵā), because you have despised me (bəzîṯānî) and have taken the wife of Uriah the Hittite.' The judicial sentence is proportional: David used the sword (through the Ammonites) to remove Uriah; the sword will not leave his house. bəzîṯānî (you have despised me) intensifies the accusation of v9: despising YHWH's word is identified as despising YHWH himself. The decree is prospective, spanning the rest of the narrative in 2 Samuel and into Kings.
11: 'Behold, I will raise up evil against you from your own house (mēḇêṯəḵā).' The instrument of judgment will come from within David's own family — the most painful possible form of retribution. rāʿāh here means both moral evil and calamity/disaster — the word encompasses both the wickedness that will arise from David's house and the suffering it will cause him. The fulfillment is Amnon's violation of Tamar (ch13), Absalom's murder of Amnon (13:28-29), and Absalom's rebellion (chs15-18).
12: 'For you did it secretly (bassāṯer), but I will do this thing before all Israel and before the sun (negeḏ kol-yiśrāʾēl wənegeḏ haššāmeš).' The public/private contrast is the precise inverse of David's concealment strategy. The private sin — the letter of Uriah's death handed to him (11:14), the quiet appropriation of Bathsheba — is answered by public exposure. Absalom's fulfillment of v12 is narrated explicitly in 16:22 with the phrase leeney kol-yisrael (before all Israel).
13: ḥāṭāʾtî laYHWH: 'I have sinned against YHWH.' David's confession is three Hebrew words — the shortest and most direct confession in the OT. Nathan responds: gam-YHWH heʿĕḇîr ḥaṭṭāʾṯəḵā lō ṯāmûṯ (YHWH has also caused your sin to pass over — you will not die). The verb heʿĕḇîr — Hiphil of ʿāḇar (to pass over/cause to cross) — is a forensic term for the removal of judicial guilt. Ps 51, which the superscription assigns to this moment, expands the same act with vocabulary like māḥāh (blot out) and kāḇas (wash). The forgiveness is real and immediate; its consequences are not thereby cancelled (v14).
14: 'Nevertheless, because by this deed you have utterly scorned YHWH (nāʾōṣ niʾaṣtā), the child who is born to you shall die.' The construction nāʾōṣ niʾaṣtā — infinitive absolute + finite verb — is an intensification formula meaning complete, thorough contempt. The verb nāʾaṣ is stronger than the bāzâ of v9; it means to blaspheme, to treat with the utmost contempt. The child's death is the consequence that vindicates YHWH's honor before the enemies who would use David's sin as occasion to blaspheme (yənaʾăṣû). The moral calculus is not that the child is punished in David's place but that the death addresses the public dishonor done to YHWH's name.
15: 'YHWH struck the child that Uriah's wife bore to David, and it became sick.' The identification as 'Uriah's wife' rather than Bathsheba — even after she is now David's wife — echoes Nathan's accusation and keeps the moral frame visible throughout the narrative. The child's illness connects the judicial decree (v14) to observable providence.
16: 'David therefore sought God (waybaqqeš dāwid ʾeṯ-hāʾelōhîm) on behalf of the child.' The verb bāqaš (to seek urgently/earnestly) combined with fasting (wayyāṣom) and prostration (wəšākaḇ ʾārṣâ, lying on the ground) depicts intercessory prayer at its most intense. The change of divine name — YHWH in v13-15, ʾelōhîm here — may reflect David's posture of supplication before the sovereign God rather than the covenantal name.
17: 'The elders of his house stood beside him to raise him from the ground, but he would not, nor did he eat food with them.' The contrast between the elders' attempts and David's refusal dramatizes the depth of his intercession. The communal pressure to resume normal life — eating, rising — is resisted as long as the child lives. David's persistence in prayer is the model of the intercessor who will not stop until the outcome is known.
18: 'On the seventh day the child died. And the servants of David were afraid to tell him that the child was dead.' The seventh-day death has covenantal resonance — the number of completion. The servants' fear (yārēʾû) to tell David reveals their uncertainty about his response: if he was so grieved while the child lived, what might he do when the child dies? Their reasoning (v18b) anticipates something extreme.
19: 'But David saw that his servants were whispering together. And David understood that the child was dead.' David's perceptive discernment — reading the behavior of those around him to infer the fact before it is spoken — is a characteristic Davidic quality throughout 2 Samuel. wayyāḇen dāwid (David understood/discerned) uses the verb bîn (to discern, to understand by observing), the same root used in Prov 1:2-6 for wisdom.
20: 'Then David arose from the earth and washed and anointed himself and changed his clothes. And he went into the house of YHWH and worshiped. He then went to his own house. And when he asked, they set food before him, and he ate.' The sequence — arose, washed, anointed, changed, worshiped — is the reversal of mourning ritual (which involved fasting, uncleanliness, torn garments). The order of David's actions is theologically significant: he goes to the house of YHWH before his own house; worship precedes comfort. The servants' astonishment (v21) shows this reversal was counterintuitive.
21: 'His servants said to him: What is this thing that you have done? You fasted and wept for the child while he was alive; but when the child died, you arose and ate food.' The servants' question opens the space for David's theology of prayer and providence. Their categories — fasting-while-alive, eating-after-death — are the conventional ones; David's explanation reverses the logic.
22: 'I fasted and wept, for I said, Who knows whether YHWH will be gracious to me (mî yōdēaʿ yaḥannēnî YHWH), that the child may live?' The phrase mî yōdēaʿ (who knows?) expresses epistemic humility before YHWH's freedom to be gracious. The same formula occurs in Joel 2:14 and Jonah 3:9 — a pattern where mî yōdēaʿ functions as the basis for penitential prayer: YHWH is free to relent; therefore we ask. The verb yāḥan (to be gracious) is from the same root as ḥen (grace/favor) — David appealed to YHWH's character of favor.
23: ʾănî hōlēk ʾēlāyw: 'I shall go to him, but he will not return to me.' This is one of the earliest expressions of personal eschatological hope in the OT. The certainty that the child will not return (lō-yāšûḇ) is paired with a prospective confidence: I am going to him (hōlēk ʾēlāyw). The verb hālak (to go) means here to die and arrive where the dead go — David's implicit belief in a post-death realm where personal continuity and reunion are possible. The statement is not merely resignation; it is oriented anticipation.
24: 'David comforted his wife Bathsheba, and went in to her and lay with her, and she bore a son, and he called his name Solomon (šəlōmōh).' The name šəlōmōh is connected to šālôm (peace/wholeness) — the child of restoration carries the peace-name. The same root connects to Solomon's building of the Temple (1 Chr 22:9: a man of peace) and to the concept of completeness/fulfillment that his reign will represent. The restored relationship between David and Bathsheba produces the covenant-continuation heir.
25: 'YHWH loved him (wayyeʾăhāḇēhû YHWH) and sent a message by Nathan the prophet. So he called his name Jedidiah (yəḏîḏyāh), because of YHWH.' The name yəḏîḏyāh (beloved of YHWH) is YHWH's own naming of the child, transmitted through the same prophet who pronounced judgment. Nathan's second appearance in ch12 is an act of grace — the prophet of judgment becomes the prophet of divine love for the new child. The divine name yāḏîḏ (beloved) belongs to the root of intimate affection; it appears in Ps 60:5 and Ps 127:2 (he grants sleep to his beloved).
26: 'Now Joab fought against Rabbah of the Ammonites and captured the royal city.' Rabbah was the Ammonite capital (modern Amman, Jordan). The battle that David had abandoned to commit adultery and arrange Uriah's murder is now completed by Joab, who summons David to claim the victory. The narrative structure places the moral crisis of chs 11-12 within the frame of the Ammonite war — the war that David missed (11:1) and that is now resolved after the judgment (12:26-31).
27: 'And Joab sent messengers to David and said, I have fought against Rabbah; moreover, I have taken the city of waters.' Joab's message is strategically diplomatic: he has taken the water supply (the fortified water source that the main city depended on), ensuring David's victory if he comes now. Joab explicitly credits the final conquest to David — pen-ʾeqqaḥ ʾănî ʾeṯ-hāʿîr wəniqrāʾ šəmî ʿāleyhā (lest I take the city and my name be called on it, v28). The general defers the honor to the king.
28: 'Now therefore gather the rest of the people together and encamp against the city and take it, lest I take the city and it be called by my name.' Joab's concern that the city not be named after him reflects ANE custom of attributing military victories to the king (the naming convention). His deference protects David's honor and the political narrative — even after the moral failure of ch11, David's kingship requires a public victory to consolidate.
29: 'So David gathered all the people and went to Rabbah and fought against it and took it.' The restoration of David to his military role marks the return to narrative normalcy. The intervening chapters (11:2–12:25) have been a parenthesis within the Ammonite war frame — the war resumes and concludes as if it had continued normally, even though everything between its opening and close has been transformed by the crisis.
30: 'And he took the crown of their king from his head. The weight of it was a talent of gold, and in it was a precious stone, and it was placed on David's head.' The Ammonite king's crown transferred to David's head is the material embodiment of the military and political victory. A talent of gold (approximately 75 lbs) would be an actual weight; commentators note this may be a ceremonial crown placed briefly on David's head rather than worn habitually. The Hebrew malkām may be the name of the Ammonite god Milcom rather than 'their king' — if so, the crown of the Ammonite deity is placed on the Israelite king, a statement of YHWH's supremacy over the foreign god.
31: 'And he brought out the people who were in it and set them to labor with saws and iron picks and iron axes and made them toil at the brick kilns. And thus he did to all the cities of the Ammonites.' The treatment of the Ammonites — wayyāśem bammēgērâ... ûbəḥārūṣê habbarzel (he set them to saws and iron threshing sledges) — is the forced labor characteristic of conquest. The textual tradition is debated: some manuscripts read 'he sawed them with saws' (a harsher interpretation); the MKT follows the sense of forced labor rather than dismemberment, consistent with David's generally measured treatment of defeated peoples.
